Prep for my development conversation with R.

Where I've landed: I do want to head toward PM — but I want to say why, and where I'm genuinely unsure, rather than just claim I'm ready.

Working the honest questions against my own three years:
- Do I want the judgment and people work over the artifact craft? Mostly yes. I love a clean status report, but the moments that have stayed with me are the judgment ones — watching you read that sponsor room, the Vantage escalation. They energise me rather than drain me, which I take as a real signal.
- Do I want the accountability? This is the honest gap. I've never been the one the SC waits on, or the one a wrong call lands on publicly. I'm drawn to it but I haven't felt it, and I'd be guessing if I told you I'm sure. I'd rather find out on something small than discover it on something large.
- Political exposure / being publicly wrong? The rooms I've observed have felt more interesting than exhausting to me — but again, observing isn't carrying.

What I understand about the change: PM isn't a bigger coordinator job, it's a different one. I support and produce; a PM owns the outcome and answers to the sponsor. Being a good coordinator is necessary but not sufficient — it gives me the foundation (reliability, written voice, pattern-reading) but the new muscles are sponsor management, strategic framing, and holding a project together when it's going badly. Those have been your job, watched from the side; I need reps, not just observation.

What I'd ask you for — concrete and sized small:
1. Let me own one small thing end-to-end — a low-risk internal initiative or a sub-workstream — with real accountability, not just delivery. That's the only way to test how I handle owning it.
2. Hand me a couple of stretch pieces of your work to draft and run: the next sponsor pre-read (you review), and one steering pre-brief.
3. I'd like to keep observing you intentionally — could I debrief with you for ten minutes after the next two sponsor interactions, on the moves you made and why?
4. I'm going to ask Maya (former PM, now a director elsewhere) to be a light mentor — someone a rung up, outside our line.

My realism about the costs: if I do this, I'm signing up for the accountability and the political weight, not just the title — and if, after owning something small, I find I don't want that, staying a senior coordinator is a real and respectable choice, not a failure. Either way I'd rather decide it on evidence than on the assumption that PM is just "up."

So my ask today isn't a title — it's the reps that will tell both of us whether the title makes sense in a year.
